items.title: Adaptation Does Not Guarantee Resistance To Extinction
items.body: Futuyma's macroevolution and diversity chapters support a guardrail for adaptation: local adaptedness does not mean a lineage evolves to avoid extinction, because environmental change, habitat loss, random origination and extinction, and lineage-level sorting can shape survival independently of organism-level fit.

review.notes: Use as an advanced adaptation/macroevolution guardrail. It should not weaken the basic claim that selection can improve fit to local environments; it separates local adaptedness from lineage persistence.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Lineage persistence depends on origination and extinction rates as well as organism-level adaptation; a constant extinction hazard can coexist with local adaptive improvement.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: selection acts within populations on heritable variation
lineage survival can depend on environmental and habitat context
speciation and extinction rates can shape macroevolutionary patterns
random extinction means not cause-free extinction, but extinction modeled without a consistent association to the trait being explained
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Adaptation is local and historical, while extinction risk can be governed by broader ecological and lineage-level circumstances.

items.title: Adaptation Often Requires Multidimensional Compromise
items.body: Pianka supports presenting adaptation as conformity to many environmental dimensions at once, where physical conditions, competitors, predators, prey, and energetic tradeoffs can require compromise rather than perfection.

review.notes: Use as a guard against single-axis or perfectionist accounts of adaptation.

items.title: Adaptation Claims Need Evidence And A Clear Level Of Selection
items.body: Williams supports a Notebook guardrail that adaptation should not be invoked casually: a proposed function should be tied to evidence of design by selection and assigned only to the level of organization demanded by the evidence.

review.notes: Use as a misconception guard for adaptation and natural-selection pages; avoid stronger public wording until additional source review.

items.title: Spatial Structure Can Change Coexistence Predictions
items.body: Wilson's gynodioecy model shows why adaptation and coexistence claims need explicit spatial assumptions: a homogeneous nonspatial model can predict extinction, while a spatially explicit simulation with local pollen movement, seed dispersal, death, and recolonization can maintain both female and hermaphroditic plant types.

review.notes: Use as an advanced adaptation/ecology modeling guardrail. Keep public treatment focused on model assumptions and spatial structure; do not promote the C code as current software practice.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room spatial simulation from biological assumptions and expected comparisons; avoid direct C port
algorithm_translation.model_focus: spatially explicit coexistence versus homogeneous mixing in a plant mating-system model

copyright_review.risk_reducer: clean-room JavaScript model using paraphrased rules and independent implementation

expected_output_checks: Compare homogeneous mixing against spatially explicit runs before claiming coexistence.
Record pollen distance, seed distance, death rate, and initial spatial distribution for each run.
Treat coexistence as a model result requiring sensitivity checks, not as a general fact about all gynodioecious systems.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare a nonspatial two-type density model with a spatially explicit model where pollen and seed dispersal are local and occupancy is cell-based.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from source equations and simulation rules; no direct equation reuse in public copy
assumptions: female plants require hermaphrodite pollen to reproduce
female seed production can exceed hermaphrodite seed production when male function is lost
homogeneous mixing removes local spatial correlations
spatial simulations can maintain coexistence through local extinction and recolonization fronts
model conclusions depend on pollen dispersal, seed dispersal, mortality, and boundary conditions
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: A trait's apparent advantage can depend on local interaction structure, not only on average performance in a well-mixed population.

notation_mapping.meaning: density or count of hermaphroditic plants
density or count of female plants
pollen imported to a location at a time
fraction of pollen from one location dispersed to another

items.title: Spatial Structure Can Change A Mating-System Model
items.body: Wilson's gynodioecy treatment supports an advanced Notebook example in which a nonspatial model and a spatial simulation can predict different outcomes for female and hermaphroditic plant types, because local replacement and expansion fronts change coexistence conditions.

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR and paired with a spatial-structure coexistence claim. Keep as an advanced adaptation/ecology modeling example rather than a foundation-ring requirement.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room spatial model from reviewed biological assumptions; avoid direct C port
algorithm_translation.model_focus: spatial coexistence versus homogeneous mixing in a gynodioecy model

expected_answer_path: gynodioecy definition
cytoplasmic male sterility inheritance
why female seed advantage can destabilize a homogeneous model
how local pollen and seed dispersal create spatial structure
why local extinction and expansion-front dynamics can maintain global coexistence
why parameter and sensitivity checks are required before biological generalization
coverage_probe.coverage_note: The prerequisite biology is now represented by the paired spatial-structure coexistence claim.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare a well-mixed two-type plant model with a spatially explicit cellular simulation whose local pollen and seed dispersal rules can produce different coexistence outcomes.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from source equations and simulation prose
assumptions: female and hermaphroditic types differ in reproductive allocation
female reproduction requires pollen from hermaphrodites
homogeneous mixing removes spatial correlations
local dispersal can make global persistence depend on expansion fronts and recolonization
simulation outcomes depend on pollen distance, seed distance, mortality, initial distribution, and mixing
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The example asks learners to treat spatial structure as a biological assumption that can change the model's evolutionary conclusion.

items.title: Needed Or Advantageous?
items.body: A student says a trait evolved because a species needed it. How would you reframe the explanation in terms of advantage, evidence, and immediate differences in reproductive success?

review.notes: Misconception probe for teleological adaptation explanations; suitable for Notebook review and Didactopus sequencing.

items.title: Does Adaptation Guarantee Lineage Survival?
items.body: If natural selection can improve fit to local conditions, why does that not imply that species evolve to avoid extinction or that older species should always be safer from extinction?

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR from the scanned Futuyma volume. Keep as an advanced adaptation/macroevolution coverage probe; pair with the adaptation-extinction boundary claim before public promotion.

expected_answer_path: distinguish local adaptedness from long-term lineage persistence
explain why environmental change or habitat loss may not create a new selectable route away from extinction
distinguish individual/population-level natural selection from lineage-level differential extinction
interpret constant or stochastic extinction rates without treating extinction as cause-free
explain why evolutionary trends require population- and lineage-level explanations
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This turns the earlier macroevolution/adaptation gap into a reviewed advanced synthesis probe.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare local adaptive change within populations with lineage-level persistence shaped by origination rates, extinction rates, and differential extinction among species.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source discusses rates but no notation is normalized here
assumptions: extinction rate can be considered at a taxonomic or lineage level
adaptedness within populations does not necessarily lower extinction hazard
random extinction has causes but may be random with respect to the trait or adaptation being analyzed
macroevolutionary trends can arise from differential speciation and extinction
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The question asks learners to prevent adaptation language from becoming a claim of inevitable progress or guaranteed survival.

items.title: When Should Competition Produce Character Displacement?
items.body: Two related species use similar food resources where each lives alone, but they differ where they live together. In another pair, the species coexist by foraging in different microhabitats without obvious mouthpart divergence. What evidence would support character displacement, and why should not every coexistence case be expected to show the same morphological change?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's competition and ecological niche chapter. Keep as an evidence-boundary question: character displacement is a testable pattern, not a blanket expectation for every coexistence or invasion case.

expected_answer_path: define character displacement as greater divergence in sympatry than in allopatry for related species
identify competition as one possible cause when the shifted trait affects resource use or niche overlap
explain that behavioral, temporal, or microhabitat separation can reduce competition without a matching morphological shift
recognize ecological release as expansion when competitors are absent or reduced
explain that invasion or competitive exclusion can remove one species before natural selection produces niche separation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a nuanced adaptation-evidence item connecting competition, niche partitioning, and extinction.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Support for character displacement requires a contrast between sympatric and allopatric populations plus a mechanism linking the divergent trait to resource use, competition, or niche overlap.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an inference checklist
assumptions: sympatric and allopatric populations are comparable enough for the contrast to be meaningful
the trait difference affects ecological interaction or resource use
alternative explanations such as habitat differences, history, and sampling are considered
coexistence can arise through non-morphological niche differences
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Adaptation claims are stronger when the observed trait pattern is tied to ecological mechanism and when plausible alternatives are tested.

source_context.source_section: Competition and the Ecological Niche; Evidence from Nature
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: conceptual passage converted into an assessment question

items.title: When Similar Niches Produce Similar Forms
items.body: Two unrelated organisms from different continents look and behave similarly while occupying roughly similar ecological roles. What evidence would support interpreting this as evolutionary convergence, and what caution should you apply before calling them ecological equivalents?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's fourth-edition discussion of convergent evolution and ecological equivalents. Use as an adaptation/common-descent boundary probe.

expected_answer_path: identify independent ancestry or distant relationship as part of the convergence inference
connect similar phenotype or behavior to similar ecological role or selective regime
look for repeated correspondence across traits relevant to the niche rather than one superficial resemblance
check dissimilarities and phylogenetic evidence before treating the organisms as true ecological equivalents
distinguish convergence from homology due to recent common ancestry
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds an adaptation probe that also supports common-descent reasoning by separating convergent similarity from inherited similarity.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Similarity caused by comparable selective environments should be evaluated against phylogenetic independence and against trait differences not explained by the shared ecological role.
assumptions: the organisms' ecological roles can be compared
phylogenetic relationship or taxonomic distance is known well enough for the inference
the traits being compared are functionally relevant rather than arbitrary
differences as well as similarities are considered
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Convergence shows that adaptation can make unrelated lineages resemble one another, but it is an inference that requires phylogenetic and ecological checks.

items.title: Why Is A Jack-Of-Many-Trades Not Always Best?
items.body: A predator can evolve a narrow prey-size preference that is very efficient when that prey is common, or a broader diet that works across many prey sizes but is less efficient on any one size. How does the principle of allocation explain this tradeoff, and why should a learner connect foraging tactics to reproduction rather than treating energy intake as the only measure of fitness?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's resource budgets and allocation chapter. Keep the item focused on tradeoff reasoning: broad resource use can be adaptive under variable resources but may reduce peak performance on the most profitable resource.

expected_answer_path: state that organisms have finite time, energy, and material budgets
explain that allocation to foraging, growth, maintenance, and reproduction creates tradeoffs
interpret a utilization curve as actual resource use under specified environmental conditions
contrast specialization on the most profitable prey with generalized use of a wider prey range
recognize that energy uptake per unit time is a proxy, while lifetime reproductive success is closer to fitness
connect foraging inputs to reproductive outputs rather than optimizing each in isolation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a foraging/life-history bridge to adaptation coverage using Pianka's allocation framework.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: With a finite resource budget, increasing breadth of resource use can increase environmental coverage while lowering peak efficiency on the best resource; fitness inference requires linking input acquisition to reproductive output.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an optimization tradeoff
assumptions: time, energy, or material budgets are finite
prey availability varies across space or time
foraging efficiency and reproductive output are not automatically identical
generalist and specialist strategies are compared in the same environmental context
performance breadth and peak performance can constrain one another
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Adaptive explanations should ask what is being optimized and what is being traded away, especially when foraging, maintenance, growth, and reproduction compete for the same budget.

notation_mapping.meaning: actual use of prey or resources across available types under specified conditions
fitness-relevant output that foraging performance may influence

source_context.source_section: Resource Budgets and the Principle of Allocation
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: source-mined foraging and life-history tradeoff probe

items.title: Why Is Longer Life Not Automatically Favored?
items.body: A species in a variable, uncrowded habitat produces many small offspring early, while a related species in a crowded habitat delays reproduction and produces fewer larger offspring. What evidence would support a life-history tradeoff explanation, and why should a learner avoid saying that longer life or higher fecundity is automatically favored by selection?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's life-history passages. Keep the item as a tradeoff and fitness-proxy guardrail: survival, early reproduction, fecundity, and offspring size must be interpreted through reproductive value and allocation context.

expected_answer_path: state that selection ultimately acts through differential reproductive success, not survival alone
explain that reproductive value changes with age, so early and late effects can have different selective consequences
connect high fecundity, offspring size, juvenile survivorship, and adult survivorship as allocation-linked variables
recognize that more current reproduction can reduce later survival or future reproduction
interpret r/K language as a heuristic contrast between opportunistic and equilibrium life-history tendencies, not a literal rule for every population
ask for evidence about age-specific reproduction, mortality, density, and offspring survival before assigning an adaptive explanation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed life-history tradeoff probe to adaptation coverage while linking to natural-selection and population-thinking guardrails.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For a finite allocation budget, increased current reproductive effort can trade off against future survival or reproduction; the fitness consequence depends on age-specific reproductive value, mortality, fecundity, offspring size, and density context.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an age-structured tradeoff model
assumptions: time, energy, or material budgets constrain simultaneous investment in survival and reproduction
age-specific reproduction and survival differ among life-history strategies
offspring number and offspring size can constrain one another
juvenile survivorship and density context affect the value of current reproductive effort
survival is fitness-relevant only insofar as it changes reproductive output
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Life-history explanations should compare whole reproductive schedules rather than treating long life, early reproduction, many offspring, or large offspring as universally optimal.

notation_mapping.meaning: expected contribution of an individual at a given age to future reproduction
number of offspring or reproductive output in a life-history schedule
probability of surviving across a life-history interval

source_context.source_section: Life Histories; Reproductive Value; r and K Selection
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: source-mined life-history allocation and fecundity-survivorship probe

items.title: Benefit Or Functional Design?
items.body: A group behavior appears to benefit the group. What additional evidence would be needed before treating that benefit as the evolved function of the behavior?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's social-adaptation guardrail. Use to separate an observed beneficial effect from evidence that selection designed a trait for that effect.

expected_answer_path: distinguish a beneficial effect from an evolved function
ask whether the trait shows evidence of functional design for that effect
identify the level at which selection is being claimed to act
compare group-level explanations with individual-level mechanisms that could produce the same pattern
avoid treating group benefit alone as sufficient evidence for group adaptation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens adaptation coverage by adding a Williams-derived guardrail for functional inference and level-of-selection claims.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Observed benefit is not sufficient evidence of adaptive function; adaptive-function claims require evidence that selection favored design features producing the effect at the claimed level of organization.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source argument is evidentiary rather than equation-based
assumptions: a trait can have effects that were not the target of selection
group-level outcomes can emerge from individual-level adaptive behavior
functional claims require evidence beyond usefulness or benefit
the level of selection must match the evidence being used
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Learners should ask what selection process would have built the trait, not merely whether the trait has a helpful consequence.

items.title: Why Would A Localized Clone Pay For Sex?
items.body: In Williams's strawberry-coral model, a sessile clone can spread vegetatively until environmental gradients and competitors limit it. Why might such a clone still invest in sexually produced dispersal stages even though sex diverts resources from clonal growth and pays the cost of meiosis?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's strawberry-coral model. Use as an adaptation/resource-allocation and model-evidence question rather than as a claim that all clonal organisms should invest heavily in sex.

expected_answer_path: identify the organism as a sessile or localized clone that can spread vegetatively but cannot spread indefinitely
explain that environmental gradients, competitors, grazers, parasites, and local genotype-environment interactions limit clonal territory
state the tradeoff: sexual reproduction diverts resources from maintenance and vegetative spread and still pays the cost of meiosis
explain the benefit: genetically diverse dispersal stages can place some of the clone's genes into regions where a different recombinant genotype has higher local fitness
distinguish general fitness from local fitness at a position in the habitat
ask for empirical checks such as localized clone territories, limited overlap, long persistence with fluctuating borders, and rare successful establishment from sexual seedlings
avoid assuming that sexual reproduction is favored unless genotype-environment interaction and clonal localization make dispersal with variation useful
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This extends Williams 1975 coverage from isolated clonal habitats to sessile clonal organisms in environmental gradients, linking adaptation to resource allocation and local fitness.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A localized clone can favor investment in sexual dispersal when the expected genetic survival gained by diverse propagules in new local-fitness contexts exceeds the loss in clonal maintenance, vegetative growth, and meiotic transmission.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source model normalized qualitatively; Williams represents fitness as a combination of general and local components
assumptions: clone size and persistence depend on resource allocation to maintenance and vegetative growth
sexual propagules are dispersed beyond the current clonal territory
local habitat position changes genotype-specific fitness
boundaries of current clonal spread indicate limits of the clone's genotype in nearby conditions
genotype-environment interaction is strong enough that recombined propagules can sometimes outperform the parental clone elsewhere
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex can be adaptive for a localized clone when genetic diversification helps its lineage escape the finite success of one local genotype in one local territory.

notation_mapping.meaning: fitness component averaged across representative natural conditions
fitness component specific to a position in the habitat or environmental gradient

source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source model converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977
